incorrects.description: Student selected 2, confusing items per container with container count

params.description: All 4 circles highlight to show they are the groups.

steps.text: You counted what's inside. Count the circles themselves. Each circle is a group.

incorrects.description: Student selected 6, possibly counting containers plus items

params.description: All 4 circles highlight to show they are the groups.

steps.text: Don't count the dots yet. Just the circles. How many circles do you see?

params.description: Circles highlight sequentially as they are counted: 1, 2, 3, 4.

steps.text: Let me show you. Count the circles with me: 1, 2, 3, 4. Four circles means four groups.

params.description: First circle highlights to focus attention on counting its contents.

steps.text: Look at one circle. Count the dots inside. How many dots are in this one circle?

incorrects.description: Student selected 3, miscounted items in container

params.description: First circle highlights to focus attention on counting its contents.

steps.text: Pick one circle and count carefully. Are there 2 dots or more?

incorrects.description: Student selected 4, confusing container count with items per container

params.description: First circle zooms in. Dots inside are counted: 1, 2.

steps.text: You're counting the circles. Look inside ONE circle. Count just the dots in that circle: 1, 2. Two dots in each circle.

incorrects.description: Student reversed groups and items per group

params.description: All circles highlight to reinforce container count.

steps.text: You flipped them. How many circles? That's your groups. How many dots in one circle? That's in each group.

incorrects.description: Student counted groups correctly but stated wrong items per group

params.description: First circle highlights. Dots inside pulse to draw attention.

steps.text: You have the groups right. Look inside one circle. Count the dots. Are there 4?

incorrects.description: Student miscounted both groups and items per group

params.description: Circles highlight sequentially: 1, 2, 3, 4. Then first circle zooms. Dots counted: 1, 2.

steps.text: Let's count together. Circles: 1, 2, 3, 4. Four groups. Now one circle: 1, 2 dots. Two in each. So you have 4 groups of 2.
